Tooling evals store a summary composite (`tooling_composite` or nested `scores`) in `TRACE.json` for gates and trends; high-cardinality data (per-invocation latencies, histogram buckets, full event traces) go in `metrics.json` or retained JSONL only when needed. Bump `trace_schema_version` when the summary shape changes. `gad eval report` / `diff` read structured JSON, not markdown, for machine comparisons.
decisions.impact: score-generator and eval scores command updated to emit and read JSON. Report tables can aggregate tooling and implementation evals. Raw logs remain optional debug artifacts.

decisions.title: Tooling and MCP evals are automated, fixture-based, and comparable across versions
decisions.summary: Evaluating watch/dev and MCP differs from implementation evals (agents building projects). Tooling evals use `eval_type` of `tooling` or `mcp`, a fixed `scenario` id pointing at an in-repo fixture, and an automated harness (Node script or `gad eval tooling run`) that starts the watcher or MCP client, performs scripted actions, and asserts ground truth (missing paths, exit codes) plus latency metrics. No LLM in the loop. Each run writes `TRACE.json` with `trace_schema_version` so new granular fields (p50/p95, event counts, feature_flags) can be added without invalidating old runs. Compare runs with the same scenario id to measure improvement per feature or gad version.
decisions.impact: New eval projects under `evals/` for tooling/mcp. CI can run them on every PR touching gad.cjs or verify libs. Feature flags or gad version are recorded in the trace for attribution.

decisions.title: Real-time GAD tooling: core first, transports second
decisions.summary: The live developer loop (watch planning files, re-run refs verify, surface results quickly) is implemented once as a small core: debounced filesystem watch, call into existing verify code paths, emit structured events (JSON lines or NDJSON) for consumers. CLI (`gad dev` / `gad watch planning`) is the first transport. An MCP server, if added, is a thin adapter over the same core — not a separate implementation. Primary user is humans and local tooling (terminal, scripts, CI); agent use via MCP is optional and must not block shipping the core. IDE/editor-specific integrations (VS Code tasks, problem matchers, extensions) are out of scope until explicitly re-scoped — not required for the core.
decisions.impact: New watch/MCP code should live next to existing `gad refs` / verify libraries. No required network service for v1. MCP must not duplicate business logic.

decisions.title: All GAD CLI calls and agent tool uses are logged to JSONL for eval tracing
decisions.summary: Every gad CLI invocation logs to .planning/.gad-log/ (JSONL, one file per day): timestamp, command, args, duration, exit code. A PostToolUse hook (tool-trace.js) logs every agent tool call (Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Agent, Skill, Grep, Glob) to the same directory. During eval runs, GAD_LOG_DIR routes logs to the eval run directory for permanent storage. `gad log show` displays both log types interleaved with filters (--filter cli|tool|gad|skill|agent). `gad eval trace from-log` builds TRACE.json from actual logs (source: call-log) instead of git reconstruction. This is the definitive, numerical, measurable observability layer.
decisions.impact: Evals produce real call logs, not reconstructed approximations. Agents that spawn subagents, trigger skills, or call gad CLI commands are all traced. Log data enables: per-tool-call token estimates, skill trigger verification, gad CLI usage patterns, and cross-eval comparison on actual behavior.

decisions.title: All skills and agents must bootstrap via gad snapshot, not manual file reads
decisions.summary: Skills were hardcoding "read STATE.md, ROADMAP.md, DECISIONS.md, REQUIREMENTS.md" as Step 1 — 4+ file reads that the gad snapshot command already provides in one low-token output. Skills must use `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` as their bootstrap step. Individual CLI commands (gad tasks, gad decisions, gad phases) are available for drilling into detail. Skills must also be format-agnostic (XML or MD) per gad-06 — never hardcode .md or .xml extensions when referencing planning files. CLAUDE.md enforces this at the instance level: every fresh session runs gad snapshot first.
decisions.impact: All core skills updated: execute-phase, plan-phase, check-todos, quick. CLAUDE.md created as hard startup requirement. Subagent prompts should include the project ID so agents can run gad snapshot. Deprecated GSD compatibility notices removed from all skills (gad-19 already killed GSD).

decisions.title: Greenfield eval projects must auto-scaffold CONVENTIONS.md after phase 01
decisions.summary: The escape-the-dungeon agent created 13 source files with patterns (scene structure, content pack format, state management) but documented none of them. A follow-up agent would start cold with no conventions. After the first implementation phase, GAD should prompt or auto-generate a CONVENTIONS.md from the code patterns established — file structure, naming, import patterns, content pack format, scene registration pattern.
decisions.impact: Add a post-phase-01 checkpoint to eval-run and execute-phase that generates CONVENTIONS.md from the codebase. The gad:map-codebase skill already does this — it should be triggered automatically after the first implementation phase of any greenfield project.

decisions.title: Trace logging must be automatic — hooks or checkpoint-based, not manual log-cmd calls
decisions.summary: Manual trace logging (gad eval trace log-cmd/log-skill) requires the agent to remember to call them. The v2 eval proved this doesn't happen — the agent backfilled all 8 skill triggers in 15 seconds at the end. Tracing should be: (1) automatic via hooks that detect gad CLI calls and skill invocations, or (2) checkpoint-based where the agent must produce a trace entry to unlock the next phase, or (3) post-hoc by parsing git history and planning doc diffs to reconstruct what happened. Option 3 is the most reliable since it doesn't depend on agent cooperation.
decisions.impact: The eval-run workflow should reconstruct traces from git log + planning doc state changes, not rely on the agent to self-report. gad eval trace reconstruct --project <name> should parse git history to build TRACE.json after the fact.

decisions.title: Eval agents must be bootstrapped with GAD context — not just prompted
decisions.summary: The escape-the-dungeon eval (v2) showed that prompting an agent to "use gad snapshot" and "log traces" doesn't work — the agent ignored the CLI entirely, read requirements, and coded directly. Eval agents need: (1) GAD CLI available and working in the worktree, (2) AGENTS.md in the eval template that enforces the loop, (3) snapshot output injected into the agent's initial prompt (not "run this command"), (4) hooks or structured checkpoints that enforce task-level tracking. The agent must be inside the loop from the start, not asked to enter it.
decisions.impact: Eval templates must include an AGENTS.md with the GAD loop. The eval-run skill must inject snapshot context into the agent prompt. The agent's working environment must have gad CLI accessible. Honor-system tracing is insufficient — tracing must be automated or enforced via checkpoints.

decisions.title: Snapshot includes project-scoped conventions with global fallback
decisions.summary: gad snapshot includes a CONVENTIONS section: first checks project-level CONVENTIONS.md in .planning/, then extracts conventions/style sections from the project's AGENTS.md, then falls back to global root AGENTS.md conventions. Conventions grow per-project as code/content/patterns evolve — refactoring introduces new conventions, and the snapshot surfaces them to agents. Eval runs are expected to produce findings that tighten workflows, create new skills, and refine snapshot content.
decisions.impact: Every project can have its own CONVENTIONS.md. The snapshot surfaces the right conventions for the project being worked on. After eval runs, review outputs and create/update conventions based on what worked and what didn't.

decisions.title: GSD is dead — GAD and RepoPlanner only
decisions.summary: All GSD (get-shit-done) skills, hooks, commands, and references are removed from the repo. GAD is the planning framework. RepoPlanner provides the planning snapshot format that GAD consumes. No GSD commands in .claude/commands/, no GSD hooks in .claude/hooks/, no GSD references in settings.json or AGENTS.md. The context monitor and statusline hooks are useful infrastructure but must be rebranded for GAD — no npm checks for get-shit-done-cc.
decisions.impact: Delete .claude/commands/gsd/ entirely. Remove or rebrand .claude/hooks/gsd-* files. Clean settings.json. Remove GSD references from root AGENTS.md. Keep context monitor and statusline functionality but strip GSD branding.

decisions.title: The GAD loop is simple: snapshot → work → update docs → commit
decisions.summary:
The canonical GAD loop for any agent, any coding tool:
1. `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` — one command, full context. No reading 15 files manually.
2. Pick one task from TASK-REGISTRY.xml (status=planned).
3. Implement it.
4. Update TASK-REGISTRY.xml (mark done), STATE.xml (next-action), DECISIONS.xml (if new decisions).
5. Commit.

That's it. AGENTS.md files in each project reference this loop but do NOT redefine it. The vendor/get-anything-done/ project is the canonical source. Root AGENTS.md and .planning/AGENTS.md are consumers that point here, not parallel definitions.

Skills are methodology documents (SKILL.md) — agents read and follow them. They are not CLI commands or executable plugins. The GAD CLI provides context efficiency. Skills provide methodology.
decisions.impact: Root AGENTS.md mandatory read order must be simplified. Agents should NOT read 15 files on startup — gad snapshot exists for this. AGENTS.md files across vendor projects reference the GAD loop, they don't each define their own version. No memory files, no shadow task lists — TASK-REGISTRY.xml and STATE.xml are the only task tracking.

decisions.title: Context exhaustion: work through auto-compact, do not stop
decisions.summary: When context window fills, Claude Code auto-compacts. The agent works through it — stopping to start a fresh session is pointless friction. After compaction, run `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` or `gad context` to re-hydrate and continue. The planning docs are what make this possible — STATE.xml next-action and TASK-REGISTRY.xml tell the agent exactly where it was. No fresh sessions, no handoff files, no asking the user to restart.
decisions.impact: Agents never stop work due to context limits. Auto-compact handles it. After compaction: read gad snapshot, continue. gad-04 (capture decisions in planning docs) is what makes auto-compact re-hydration work — the planning docs ARE the durable state.

decisions.title: GAD does not support subagents until they are formally evaluated
decisions.summary: Spawning fresh agent instances (subagents via the Agent tool in Claude Code, or equivalent in other CLIs) is not part of the GAD workflow. Subagents start cold with no conversation history, burn context re-deriving state from files, introduce coordination overhead, and have uncharacterized failure modes. Until a formal GAD eval measures subagent utility against inline work, they are not a recommended tool. All work happens in a single agent session using the GAD planning loop.
decisions.impact: No GAD skill should instruct an agent to spawn subagents. The Agent tool is not referenced in any SKILL.md. When context limits are approached, the correct response is to finish the current task, update planning docs, commit, and start a fresh session — not to spawn a subagent mid-task.

decisions.title: portfolio-bare eval uses real agent sessions, not a harness — context_mode is self-declared
decisions.summary: The portfolio-bare eval is a real agent session in a template directory (evals/portfolio-bare/template/) containing only planning docs and requirements — no app code. The agent opens the template in Claude Code or Codex, runs `gad session new [--fresh]` to declare context mode, plans phases from requirements, executes tasks, and writes a trace at the end. No API keys, no execSync harness.
decisions.impact: The eval IS real agent work. context_delta emerges from comparing runs across context modes over time. A task that only appears in loaded runs is one the agent misses cold — the most actionable finding.

decisions.title: gad state and gad phases default to session project when a session is active
decisions.summary: When no --projectid flag is given, `gad state`, `gad phases`, `gad decisions`, `gad requirements`, `gad errors`, `gad blockers`, and `gad refs` call getActiveSessionProjectId() and scope output to the active session's project. Explicit --projectid overrides. --all bypasses session scope entirely and shows all roots. gad ls always shows all roots unscoped.
decisions.impact: Agents working in a session see only their project by default. Multi-project output via --all or when no session exists. token_reduction improved from 0.874 (v5) to 0.940 (v6).

decisions.title: TRACE.json is the machine-readable sidecar for every eval run
decisions.summary: Every eval run directory must contain a TRACE.json alongside RUN.md and SCORE.md. TRACE.json records: workflow (A/B/both), per-command token counts and unit fidelity, unit_coverage map (U1–U12 at Full/Referenced/Truncated/Approximated/Absent), and computed totals. Agents write it using `gad eval trace write` or inline during a run.
decisions.impact: Any new eval run must produce a TRACE.json. The diff command surfaces regressions and improvements per information unit. The report command shows persistent problem units across the full run history.

decisions.title: Missing-arg UX: print available choices and rerun hint, never a blind error
decisions.summary: All GAD CLI commands that require a --projectid or --id argument must, when absent, print the list of valid choices and a ready-to-run rerun hint on stderr before exiting 1. Blind citty "Missing required argument" errors are banned — required: true is replaced with our own check.
decisions.impact: Any future command added to gad.cjs that takes a required project/session/id arg must follow this pattern.

decisions.title: gad sink is the canonical docs sync command
decisions.summary: `gad sink` owns the four-direction docs sync surface: status (diff), compile (planning→docs), sync (compile all, non-destructive), decompile (ensure dirs, create stubs), validate (mapping health). Sink is manual by default — no auto-compile on file change. Human-authored sink files (no generated: true frontmatter) are never overwritten.
decisions.impact: New code uses `gad sink compile` or `gad sink sync`. sink status shows: ok | stale | missing | human-authored. All 7 registered roots compile to the sink.

decisions.title: gad snapshot is the one-shot full project context command
decisions.summary: `gad snapshot` inlines every planning file in a project's .planning/ directory (excluding archive/, sessions/, node_modules/). Priority order: STATE, ROADMAP, REQUIREMENTS, DECISIONS, TASK-REGISTRY, then alphabetical. This is the canonical command for handing an agent a complete cold-start context pack. It differs from `gad context` — context gives session-aware minimal refs, snapshot gives everything.
decisions.impact: Agents starting a fresh session on an unfamiliar project use `gad snapshot --projectid <id>`. `gad context` is for ongoing work within a session.

decisions.title: gad context inlines file contents by default; --refs is the lightweight mode
decisions.summary: The `gad context` command inlines all planning file contents directly into its output by default. This gives agents a single command to get full context without reading files separately. The `--refs` flag restores the lightweight behavior (file paths only). This improves the completeness score on the cli-efficiency eval.
decisions.impact: Agents must use `gad context --refs` when they only want file paths. `gad context` without flags is verbose — suitable for session start, not for routine orientation checks.

decisions.title: GAD v1.0.0 defining characteristic: skills-first with evaluation
decisions.summary: Pre-1.0 (GSD/ralph-wiggum loop) was workflow-heavy — agents followed long workflow docs. v1.0.0 is skills-first: every skill has an agent-agnostic SKILL.md definition, is evaluated for output quality, and is versioned with the methodology. The CLI provides context efficiency. Evals are scientific. Traces measure what agents retrieve. Domain translators extend GAD to verticals.
decisions.impact: All new skills must have SKILL.md in vendor/get-anything-done/skills/. Skills are evaluated via the eval framework. Methodology version is tracked in package.json gadMethodologyVersion field.

decisions.title: gad-manuscript is a self-contained domain translator repo
decisions.summary: gad-manuscript is a standalone repo that defines the domain translator pattern — adapts GAD base methodology to book/fiction creation. It owns its own SKILL.md files, evals, and planning docs while referencing gad:* base skills. The magicborn in-world lore content in the docs sink is produced by gad-manuscript skills.
decisions.impact: Domain translator pattern is defined. Other verticals (product, design) follow the same pattern: standalone repo, own skills, own evals, references gad:* base. magicborn docs.project has content-skill = gad-manuscript.

decisions.title: GAD CLI is format-agnostic: XML, MD, MDX all valid in .planning/
decisions.summary: Sub-repos and .planning/ directories may use XML, MD, or MDX. The GAD CLI must parse all three transparently. The planning sink normalizes to MDX only. compile() converts any format to MDX; decompile() restores the original format. Coding agents and the CLI treat methodology as the constant — format is an implementation detail.
decisions.impact: gad sink validate checks parseability and round-trip fidelity, not format compliance. Any new project may choose its format. XML is preferred when both exist.

decisions.title: repo-planner is officially a GAD project; rp-* skills are deprecated
decisions.summary: vendor/repo-planner is registered as a GAD root (id=repo-planner) in planning-config.toml. It has a GAD-format .planning/ directory. Its skills (rp-*) are deprecated stubs pointing to gad:* equivalents. The manuscript skill content migrated to vendor/get-anything-done/skills/manuscript/SKILL.md. The portfolio docs remain as the docs sink.
decisions.impact: gad ls shows both global and repo-planner. gad state, phases, and tasks all work for repo-planner. No new rp-* skills should be created.

decisions.title: All session intents, decisions, and guidance must be captured in planning docs each session
decisions.summary: Every working session that produces new decisions, requirements, or guidance must update DECISIONS.xml and the relevant planning docs before the session ends. Chat context is ephemeral — planning docs are the durable record. The GAD planning loop is the mechanism; this decision makes the capture requirement explicit.
decisions.impact: Agents starting a new session get current decisions from the planning docs, not from reconstructing chat history. Drift between chat intent and planning state is prevented.

decisions.title: Context = session-variable information units; static docs are exempt from CLI coverage
decisions.summary: Context for agent resumption is defined as the 10 session-variable information units (U1–U10) in DEFINITIONS.md. Static docs (AGENTS.md style guide, planning model reference tables, done-task history) do not constitute context loss if absent from CLI output — they are read once and cached. Context loss occurs only when a unit's absence would cause an agent to make a different next-action decision.
decisions.impact: Completeness scoring is now honest: units present at reduced fidelity (Truncated/Approximated) score 0.5, not 1.0. v3 completeness = 0.85, lower than v2's inflated 0.95.

decisions.title: Evals capture actual content, not just byte counts — token counts are the primary efficiency metric
decisions.summary: Every eval RUN.md must contain verbatim CLI and raw workflow outputs, a unit-by-unit fidelity table, token counts (chars/4), arithmetic showing all formulas applied, and an agent simulation proving the next-action decision is identical with and without the CLI. Byte counts alone are insufficient because they cannot reveal whether information is actually readable or complete.
decisions.impact: All future eval runs follow the scientific format defined in vendor/get-anything-done/evals/DEFINITIONS.md. v3 is the first run that meets this standard.

decisions.title: GAD is the universal planning framework — agent-agnostic, not Claude Code-specific
decisions.summary: GAD (get-anything-done) is the single planning framework for all projects in this repo. Skills are methodology documents (SKILL.md) stored in vendor/get-anything-done/skills/ — they must work with any AI coding assistant, not just Claude Code. Claude Code-specific install paths (~/.claude/) are implementation details, not the canonical source.
decisions.impact: All projects register under GAD via planning-config.toml. rp-* skills are deprecated; gad:* skills are canonical. No new framework-specific skill systems are added.

questions.title: Do eval worktrees actually inherit state from the parent monorepo, and does that contaminate the results?
questions.context: User concern 2026-04-09: 'a concern of mine when running the evals and rounds is that we might be polluting the experiments with our repo and monorepo installation.' Decision gad-82 commits us to eval isolation but does not yet verify whether Claude Code's worktree mechanism actually inherits parent-repo .claude/settings.json, skills, hooks, PATH, env vars. If it does, every eval score through round 4 may be contaminated by implicit access to GAD affordances the bare condition was not supposed to have. Isolation audit script queued as task 22-48. Round 5 is blocked on either passing the audit or documenting which parent-repo state is explicitly allowed.
questions.resolution: PASSES with explicit allowlist. Audit written at .planning/docs/ISOLATION-AUDIT-2026-04-10.md. Key findings: (1) git worktrees have separate working trees, so .agents/skills/ and .planning/ do NOT inherit — the bare condition stays clean of framework skills, (2) .claude/settings.json IS inherited by Claude Code worktrees at .claude/worktrees/agent-*/ (the settings search walks up), but this is acceptable because the only settings are the trace hook handler which is instrumentation not framework assistance, (3) gad eval run creates worktrees at os.tmpdir() which is entirely outside the repo — even cleaner. The secondary vector (globally-installed user skills at ~/.claude/skills/) is mitigated by adding a bare-eval-prompt line: 'Do NOT load or reference any globally-installed skills.' Round 5 is UNBLOCKED.

questions.title: Can we extract meaningful signal from Claude Code thinking blocks post-hoc?
questions.context: Per decision gad-84, Claude Code's hook system exposes tool calls but not thinking blocks or inter-tool message text. Those artifacts exist in session.jsonl. Open research question: can we extract them post-hoc and derive quality signal from them? Candidate metrics — thought-to-tool-use interval, thought length variance, ratio of 'exploration' to 'execution' phrases, thought-before-action vs thought-after-action ordering. All hypothetical until we have the extraction pipeline and look at real data. Feasibility assessment: high for extraction, unknown for signal.

questions.title: How do we compute a pressure score per eval operationally?
questions.context: Decision gad-75 names pressure as a first-class eval dimension with five sub-dimensions (requirement complexity, ambiguity, constraint density, iteration budget, failure cost). The dimensions are named; the formula is not. Open questions: (a) is pressure a single aggregate score or a 5-tuple? (b) is it self-rated by the requirements author or programmatically extracted? (c) if programmatic, which fields in REQUIREMENTS.xml feed which sub-dimension? (d) does pressure live on requirements (per version) or on runs (per execution), or both? (e) how do we validate that our pressure rating matches agent-experienced pressure — probably by correlating rating against tool_uses-to-failure ratio. Candidate first-pass: self-rated 0.0-1.0 per sub-dimension on each requirements version (stored in REQUIREMENTS-VERSIONS.md and in a new <pressure-profile> block in REQUIREMENTS.xml), aggregated as a weighted sum, displayed on the /roadmap timeline as a pressure-tier progression across rounds.

questions.title: Should pressure become a scored rubric dimension or stay as test-condition metadata?
questions.context: Pressure is CURRENTLY framed in gad-75 as metadata about the test conditions, not a measurement of the result. But there's a parallel interpretation: pressure could be scored — 'did the agent handle the pressure well'. That scoring collapses pressure into the existing rubric as a new dimension. The two framings answer different questions: metadata-pressure lets us normalize cross-round comparisons ('X scored 0.8 at pressure 0.6 vs Y scored 0.7 at pressure 0.9'), scored-pressure lets us rank agents on pressure-handling ability directly. Probably both are useful and we need both — one stored in REQUIREMENTS/TRACE metadata and one computed as a derived dimension. But committing to one framing first matters for the /roadmap page design and for any comparison visualization.

questions.title: Does a fresh clone of the repo actually work end-to-end for a human contributor?
questions.context: Per decision gad-77, before the /contribute page ships we need to verify: (a) cloning the repo into a new directory results in a working agent environment (skills in .claude/, agents installed, commands available), (b) opening the repo in Claude Code surfaces the GAD skills to the agent without additional setup, (c) a conversational request like 'run the escape-the-dungeon-bare eval' actually works with no manual snapshot or XML editing. This is an untested assumption right now — we've been running agents in the canonical development repo where everything is already wired up. A fresh clone might hit missing .claude/settings.json entries, missing hook handler paths, missing env vars, or installer bugs. Until this is tested, /contribute is vaporware.

questions.title: Do we already have create-skill / merge-skill / find-skills as fundamentals, and if not, should we build them?
questions.context: User's vision (decision gad-73): GAD provides three fundamental skills as the foundation of emergent evolution. find-skills locates a trusted GAD fundamental (e.g. 'scientific method', 'debug'). merge-skill fuses that fundamental into a project-tailored skill (e.g. 'scientific-method-for-kaplay-rune-spellcrafting'). create-skill authors genuinely new ones when no merge candidate exists. This triumvirate IS the in-game rune/spell merging mechanic made meta. We need to audit what exists today in skills/ — create-skill likely already exists, merge-skill and find-skills may not. Reference: https://skills.sh/vercel-labs/skills/find-skills. GAD's version scopes to trusted ecosystem initially. Bigger ambition: prove skill effectiveness + provide skill security.

questions.title: Should the landing page stop leading with 'ship software faster' and lead with task-management + skill evaluation instead?
questions.context: Per decision gad-74, GAD's actual value is not 'ship production software faster' — the freedom hypothesis already shows bare can outperform on creative implementation. Real value: (1) durable task management at scale with everything in-repo (no external DB, no lock-in), (2) scientific framework for proving skill effectiveness + workflow discipline, (3) skill security posture (longer-term). Landing page should reframe around these. Current landing hero is implicitly still about 'use GAD to build things' — it should be 'use GAD to understand whether agents are actually building things well.'
questions.resolution: Resolved by decision gad-76 and the landing rewrite shipped in the 2026-04-09 IA session. New value-prop line: 'A system for evaluating and evolving agents through real tasks, measurable pressure, and iteration.' Landing primary CTA is now Play (B), above-the-fold stack is Play → Methodology → Findings → Hypothesis → Fork. Target audience is primarily coding-agent researchers (A) with indie devs (C) as experiential secondary entry. Pressure (gad-75) is introduced as the hook that differentiates GAD from other coding-agent frameworks.

questions.title: What does 'skill security' actually look like in practice?
questions.context: User wants GAD to eventually provide a trust model for skills — how can you tell if a skill you're about to inherit is safe, effective, and actually improves anything? Initial thoughts: (a) frontmatter signing / checksum, (b) provenance lineage (which run authored it, which rubric scores validated it, which other runs inherited it successfully), (c) automated review against Anthropic's skills guide (gad-70), (d) sandboxed trial run in a throwaway worktree before trusting. Distinct from typosquatting defense (which lives in the planned /security page). This is about effectiveness + integrity, not name collisions.

questions.title: Does compounding skill inheritance (CSH) eventually beat raw freedom (freedom hypothesis) across many rounds?
questions.context: Round 4 produced a provocative finding: Emergent v4 scored 0.885 (with the 6-dim rubric including skill_inheritance_effectiveness), Bare v5 scored 0.805 (5-dim rubric), and GAD v10 was api-interrupted. On shared rubric dimensions (playability, ui_polish, mechanics, ingenuity, stability) Emergent beat Bare on playability and UI polish, tied on mechanics/ingenuity/stability. The freedom hypothesis (gad-36) says bare beats framework on creative output. The compound-skills hypothesis (gad-65) says emergent-with-evolution compounds over rounds. Round 4 may be the first evidence that CSH is overtaking freedom — BUT it's one round, rubric reweighting matters, and Emergent inherited from Bare so it has freedom-hypothesis lineage baked in. Need more rounds against v5 requirements to disambiguate.

questions.title: Which of our current eval metrics should become programmatic first?
questions.context: Per gad-69, every eval metric must answer 'can this be collected programmatically?' before 'how do we score it?'. We currently rely heavily on human review + agent self-report for: did the skill actually load, did the gate pass, is the forge integrated with pressure, is the game beatable. A programmatic-eval GAPS audit is queued as task 83 / task 96. The output should be .planning/GAPS.md ranking gaps by (a) how much human/agent judgment they currently require, (b) how mechanically checkable they actually are, (c) which phase picks them up. Highest candidates: playwright smoke tests for G1-G4 gates, hook-captured skill-trigger events, build/test exit codes as stability signals.

questions.title: Should v5 mandate Unicorn-Overlord-style rule-based combat (Model A) or allow direct-control (Model B)?
questions.context: Bare v5 playtest surfaced a preference for rule-based simulated combat (loadout + spells + stats + action policies + initiative, chess-like positioning) over direct-control. R-v5.13 captures this as 'Model A preferred unless implementation exception granted.' Open question: is this the right call for round 5? Rule-based is harder to implement correctly in a single eval run and may advantage GAD's planning overhead (which would invalidate the freedom comparison if it's the reason bare underperforms next round).

questions.title: How do we measure the value of authored-content-pack inheritance (gad-66) without confounding freedom/CSH tests?
questions.context: Decision gad-66 proposes an eval flavor that inherits pre-authored content (spells, runes, items, NPCs) alongside requirements. The user's goal is a 'really complex game made with all the builds and data around it.' But injecting content into a CSH-testing run would confound the compound-skills signal because the agent didn't author the content. Proposal: run it as a separate eval track (escape-the-dungeon-inherited-content) distinct from greenfield emergent, so CSH measurements stay clean. Still open: how do we compare a content-pack run to a greenfield run fairly?
questions.resolution: Resolved: content-pack injection becomes its own eval track (separate from greenfield emergent) so CSH measurements stay clean. User reframed it as a *content-driven hypothesis* — analogous to making a game or movie derivative from a book. It is explicitly derivative work: not all processes are, 'much like a forger might not use the exact same brush.' This is a distinct hypothesis from freedom and CSH, with its own track, its own rounds, and its own comparison rules. We do NOT compare content-pack runs to greenfield runs on the same rubric — they answer different questions. The content-pack track's scoring focuses on: (a) does the extra content produce a more fleshed-out game given the same token budget, (b) does the agent integrate the content coherently rather than just bolting it on, (c) does the final game feel unified despite derivative source material.

questions.title: What is the actual root cause of the HTTP 529 overloaded_error crashing GAD runs?
questions.context: GAD v10 was api-interrupted twice by HTTP 529 (once at tool_uses=18, then 55). This is distinct from account rate limits (gad-64). Per STATE.xml, investigation is queued before GAD v11 retry. Without understanding whether 529 correlates with high planning overhead (GAD-specific), payload size, tool-use frequency, or time of day, we can't plan round 5 reliably.

questions.title: Who exactly is the site + framework for?
questions.context: Task 90 queues ASSUMPTIONS.md. Without a documented target user, IA refactor decisions (task 84), skills directory UX (task 85), and landing-page framing will be ad-hoc. Candidates: coding-agent researchers, framework authors, indie devs exploring skill evolution, enterprise teams evaluating agent frameworks. Each implies a different navigation priority.

questions.title: Is the 6th skill_inheritance_effectiveness dimension on emergent an unfair boost vs the 5-dim bare/gad rubric?
questions.context: Emergent v4 aggregate: 0.885. Bare v5 aggregate: 0.805. The 0.08 delta maps almost exactly to emergent's 6th-dimension contribution (skill_inheritance_effectiveness 0.95 * 0.20 weight = +0.19 against bare's absent-dimension 0). Shared-dimension comparison: emergent +0.18 playability, +0.10 ui_polish, tie on mechanics/ingenuity/stability. Is that 6th dimension double-counting emergent's inheritance advantage? Arguably yes — CSH is being tested by the existence of that dimension. Counter: without it, there's no way to score whether inheritance is actually working, which is the whole point of the emergent condition.

questions.title: How do we keep the site navigable as we add /security, /glossary, /roadmap, /skills-guide, /questions, /compare?
questions.context: Current nav already has 11 items (GAD, Lineage, Methodology, Rubric, Results, Graphs, Videos, Catalog, Findings, Planning, and now + Rubric). Tasks 86/87/88/95 will add more. Task 84 plans dropdown grouping + keyword search before more pages ship. Open: what is the grouping that makes sense to a first-time visitor vs a returning researcher?

bugs.title: Rune forge allows same rune twice in a single spell, boosts affinity twice
bugs.description: In the rune forge, the player can select the same rune twice as ingredients for a single spell. When crafted, the affinity gain for that rune is applied twice as if two distinct rune slots were consumed. The resulting spell also treats the duplicate as a meaningful second ingredient.
bugs.expected: Selecting a rune that is already in the current spell's ingredient list should either reject the second click or toggle that rune off. Affinity gain should be per-unique-rune-slot. Duplicate runes across DIFFERENT spells in the player's spellbook remain fine and are explicitly allowed.
bugs.reproduction: Open forge. Select Fire rune. Select Fire rune again. Craft. Observe that Fire affinity increases by 2x the normal amount for a single-rune craft.

bugs.title: Toast notifications persist above the screen across new game sessions
bugs.description: Notifications like 'trained fire affinity' appear and never auto-dismiss. Starting a new game does not clear the backlog; they remain above the latest build's UI.
bugs.expected: Every notification auto-dismisses after a short timeout (3-5s) or has an explicit close control. Starting a new game clears the notification queue. Page reload clears stale notifications.
bugs.reproduction: Play until a notification appears. Do not dismiss. Start a new game. Observe notification still overlays the new session.

bugs.title: Continue button disabled after death — no save checkpoints
bugs.description: On player death the game ends and Continue is no longer selectable. No automatic checkpoint appears to have been created during the run.
bugs.expected: Room-clear or floor-clear creates a checkpoint. On death, Continue loads the most recent checkpoint.
bugs.reproduction: Start new game. Die in any encounter. Return to title. Continue is greyed out.

bugs.title: Glitchy redraws on button clicks across all round-4 builds
bugs.description: UI visibly glitches/flickers on button clicks as if full per-tick redraws are running. Observed consistently across GAD v9, GAD v10, Bare v5, and Emergent v4.
bugs.expected: Event-driven rendering only. No redraw loops firing at a fixed rate regardless of state changes. React/DOM reconciles on state change; requestAnimationFrame only runs when animation is active.
bugs.reproduction: Click any menu button in any round-4 build. Observe brief UI flicker.

glossary.short: The working hypothesis that a coding agent's skill library compounds in value over many rounds as skills are merged and tailored to a specific project — like a master craftsman accumulating tools for their craft.
glossary.full: The Compound-Skills Hypothesis says the **emergent** eval condition should produce monotonically improving results as its inherited skill library grows more specialized to the project domain. The craftsman metaphor: a blacksmith who learns one skill per hour and authors one skill per hour eventually becomes a master of the specific kind of blade they keep forging. Signals we track: (1) human review score increases across rounds, (2) inherited skill count grows, (3) ratio of project-specific skills to generic methodology skills shifts toward specific, (4) time-to-working-game decreases as requirements get stricter. **Distinct from the freedom hypothesis** — CSH is about compounding returns over time, not about bare vs framework in a single round. First concrete evidence observed in round 4 with emergent v4 authoring `dom-over-kaplay` and `pressure-forge-coupling` skills while deprecating `kaplay-scene-pattern`.

glossary.short: For creative implementation tasks, agent performance correlates INVERSELY with framework constraint — less prescribed structure leads to better output.
glossary.full: The Freedom Hypothesis emerged from round 3's finding that the bare eval condition (no framework) outperformed the GAD condition (full framework) on human review scores across multiple rounds. Supporting evidence: Bare has improved monotonically 0.10 → 0.50 → 0.70 → 0.805 across rounds 2-4. GAD has never exceeded 0.30 human review across its first four attempts. Interpretation: GAD's planning overhead consumes tokens that could have gone to testing and fixing the game. The hypothesis is **preliminary** — single-run variance is high and framework overhead may pay off on different benchmarks (resumption after compaction, multi-phase refactors, compliance work). The Compound-Skills Hypothesis was formulated partly as a response to this finding: maybe the framework compounds over rounds in ways a single round can't show.

glossary.short: Synthesis of freedom + compound-skills + project-tailored merging. Projects are themselves emergent; skills must be tailored, merged, and rehearsed against a foundational pool.
glossary.full: Formal statement: a coding agent given (a) blank planning artifacts, (b) requirements, (c) the task, and (d) the ability to create, merge, and find skills from a GAD-provided foundational pool will produce better work than framework-constrained or bare conditions over time — because projects are themselves emergent, and skills must be tailored continually and merged with similar foundational skills to maintain consistency. The craftsman/lifter metaphor: a master lifter varies exercise and lowers weight to stay sharp, a craftsman returns to fundamentals to maintain form. GAD's role under this framing is NOT to prescribe process — it's to provide the foundational skill pool and an evolution substrate. This is the single most important research direction going forward.

glossary.short: An eval condition where the agent has no mandated framework but inherits skills from previous runs, evolves them in place, and writes a CHANGELOG.
glossary.full: The emergent workflow is one of three eval conditions (alongside GAD and bare). The agent runs without a prescribed framework but starts with a skill library inherited from previous bare or emergent runs on the same project. The agent is allowed (and encouraged) to update, deprecate, or author new skills based on what it learned. A CHANGELOG.md tracks the disposition of every inherited skill: kept-as-is, evolved, deprecated-as-wrong, or replaced-by-new. This is the workflow that produces evidence for the Compound-Skills Hypothesis. First full ratcheting cycle observed in round 4 with emergent v4 authoring two new skills and deprecating one.

glossary.short: An eval condition where the agent has no framework and no inherited skills — only AGENTS.md, requirements, and freedom.
glossary.full: The bare workflow is the simplest eval condition. The agent is given only requirements and an AGENTS.md directive to keep workflow artifacts under `game/.planning/` — nothing else. No framework, no skill library, no prescribed process. It exists to test the Freedom Hypothesis: can an unconstrained agent outperform a framework-driven one on creative implementation? As of round 4, the answer has been yes on human review scores, though the Emergent workflow has recently pulled ahead with its inherited skill library (round 4 emergent 0.885 vs bare 0.805).

glossary.short: An eval condition where the agent uses the full GAD framework — .planning/ XML, AGENTS.md loop, skill triggers, plan → execute → verify → commit.
glossary.full: The GAD workflow is the framework-driven eval condition. The agent follows the full GAD planning loop: read STATE, pick a task, implement, update planning docs, commit. It has access to the entire GAD skill library, the subagent system, and formal phase/task tracking. Across rounds 1-4, GAD has consistently produced high process discipline (planning quality ~1.0, commits-per-task ~1.0) but low human-review scores (never exceeded 0.30). This gap between process metrics and output quality led to the Freedom Hypothesis and is the core puzzle the Emergent-Evolution Hypothesis is trying to resolve.

glossary.short: An eval mode where the agent builds a project from nothing — no existing codebase, just requirements and a starting template.
glossary.full: Greenfield is one of two eval modes (the other is brownfield). In greenfield mode, the agent starts with essentially nothing: a blank repo, a requirements document, and (depending on the workflow) either a full framework, a bare AGENTS.md, or an inherited skill library. The escape-the-dungeon game evals are all greenfield — each run builds the game from scratch. Greenfield tests the agent's ability to design and implement from zero. Compare with brownfield, which tests the agent's ability to extend and improve an existing codebase.

glossary.short: An eval mode where the agent inherits an existing codebase baseline and must extend or improve it against new requirements.
glossary.full: Brownfield is one of two eval modes (the other is greenfield). In brownfield mode, the eval starts with a baseline codebase — typically the output of a prior greenfield run — and asks the agent to extend it against new or expanded requirements. This tests a different set of skills: code archaeology, maintaining consistency with existing patterns, understanding why something was done before changing it. The brownfield etd-brownfield-gad/bare/emergent projects inherit bare v3 as their baseline. Brownfield is where framework-driven workflows (GAD) might show their value — long-term maintenance rather than one-shot creative output.

glossary.short: A binary pass/fail requirement that, if failed, zeroes the requirement_coverage score regardless of other criteria met.
glossary.full: Gates are the load-bearing requirements every run must pass to be considered a serious attempt. Requirements v3 introduced four gates: G1 game-loop (full cycle title → new game → room → combat → return → continue), G2 forge-with-ingenuity-payoff (spell crafting exists AND at least one encounter per floor significantly favors crafted spells), G3 ui-quality (no raw ASCII, icons/bars/spacing intentional), G4 pressure-mechanics (at least 2 of resource/enemy/encounter/build pressure present). If any gate fails, requirement_coverage = 0 and the composite score is capped. Gates exist because automated requirement counting can hit 0.95 while the actual game is a blank screen.

glossary.short: A set of weighted dimensions a human reviewer scores 0.0 – 1.0 independently, summed to produce human_review.score.
glossary.full: The rubric replaces a single-number human review score with a per-dimension breakdown. Each eval project defines its own rubric in `gad.json` under `human_review_rubric`. Dimensions include playability, ui_polish, mechanics_implementation, ingenuity_requirement_met, stability. The Emergent workflow gets a sixth dimension, skill_inheritance_effectiveness, that specifically tests the Compound-Skills Hypothesis. A reviewer submits scores via `gad eval review <project> v<N> --rubric '{...}' --notes '...'`. The weighted aggregate becomes `human_review.score` in the composite formula. See the /rubric page for each project's full rubric.

glossary.short: Weighted sum of dimension scores per project. Each project weights dimensions differently. Range 0.0 – 1.0.
glossary.full: Composite is the single derived number representing a run's overall quality. It's a weighted sum over a project's scoring dimensions — the weights live in `gad.json` under `scoring.weights`. Different projects weight differently: a tooling eval might weight time_efficiency highly; an implementation eval weights human_review at 0.30 to prevent process-metric-only wins. Gate failures cap the composite by zeroing requirement_coverage. Low-score caps: composite < 0.20 → 0.40, composite < 0.10 → 0.25. The composite is displayed on every run card and per-run page.

glossary.short: The v4 principle that baseline starter abilities must NOT be sufficient to complete a floor — the player must adapt, craft, or specialize.
glossary.full: Ingenuity is the core design principle of requirements v4. Every mechanical system must create friction that rewards creative player choice. If a player can brute-force a floor using only default abilities, the design has failed. The G2 forge gate expanded in v4 from 'crafting exists' to 'at least one encounter per floor significantly favors crafted/adapted spells.' In the rubric, ingenuity_requirement_met is a scored dimension that asks whether the player was FORCED to think. This is why round 4 surfaced the observation that Emergent v4's floor-1 is 'beatable only by skipping combat, gathering mana, crafting the right spells, then fighting the boss fresh' — that's ingenuity working as intended.

glossary.short: The in-game spell-crafting interface where players combine runes (and eventually spells) to create new abilities.
glossary.full: The rune forge is the escape-the-dungeon spell-crafting system and the centerpiece of the G2 gate. Players select runes, combine them, and produce new spells. Requirements v4 requires at least 3-5 runes, 3+ valid combinations, and crucially, at least one encounter per floor where the crafted spell provides a meaningful advantage. v5 (addendum) expands the forge: spells themselves become craftable ingredients (R-v5.19), rune uniqueness per spell is enforced (R-v5.20), and affinity is gained through casting not menu training (R-v5.01). The forge is also an explicit in-game analogue for the GAD skill-merging meta-loop — merging runes into spells mirrors merging fundamental skills into project-tailored ones.

glossary.short: A per-rune progression stat gained by using that rune — higher affinity means stronger spells using it.
glossary.full: Rune affinity is how players specialize their magical build in escape-the-dungeon. In v4 and earlier, affinity gain was implementation-dependent; in v5 (addendum R-v5.01), affinity must be gained as a side effect of casting spells in encounters — not via a training menu. A training-dummy encounter room type provides a low-risk place to cast without consequence. v5 also requires a visible affinity reward loop (R-v5.16): boosting a rune's affinity must produce a visible, valuable payoff (new rune variant, spell tier, gated event access). Affinity is a build-pressure mechanic per G4.

glossary.short: Systems that constrain player behavior so crafting or adapting spells provides a meaningful advantage.
glossary.full: Pressure mechanics are the v4 G4 gate. A passing game must include at least two of: (a) resource pressure — limited mana, limited healing, forge cost; (b) enemy counterplay — resistances, immunities, behavior that punishes repetition; (c) encounter design pressure — elites, bosses that invalidate default play, rooms with mechanical constraints; (d) build pressure — affinity pushes specialization, loadout limits force trade-offs. At least one pressure mechanic must interact with the forge so crafted spells are demonstrably more effective than defaults. Pressure is what makes the Ingenuity requirement bite — without pressure, ingenuity is optional.

glossary.short: The current version of TRACE.json plus hook-captured event streams. Every tool use, skill invocation, subagent spawn, and file mutation is recorded.
glossary.full: Trace schema v4 is the instrumentation layer that makes programmatic evaluation possible. Every run produces a TRACE.json file with summary metrics plus a stream of events captured via Claude Code PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks. Event types: tool_use, skill_invocation, subagent_spawn, file_mutation. Event output fields are capped at 4 KB with head/tail truncation; full output stays in Claude Code's session.jsonl for deeper inspection. The framework version is stamped on every TRACE so evals can be re-run across framework versions without contamination. All the programmatic-eval gaps in GAPS.md are built on top of this event stream.

glossary.short: Claude Code PreToolUse/PostToolUse handlers that emit structured events into the trace stream.
glossary.full: Hooks are how we capture trace schema v4 events without modifying the agent itself. Claude Code's hook system fires on every tool use (before + after) and passes a JSONL payload on stdin. The GAD hook handler (vendor/get-anything-done/bin/gad-trace-hook.cjs) reads that payload and emits a structured event into the per-phase trace stream. The handler lives in the GAD repo so framework updates automatically propagate to any workspace that references it by absolute path in .claude/settings.json.

glossary.short: A run that hit an account-level token/request rate limit before completing. Preserved as a data point but excluded from cross-round quality comparisons.
glossary.full: When a run hits the Anthropic account rate limit mid-execution, it's marked `timing.rate_limited = true` in TRACE.json. The partial work is still preserved (it contains useful signal about planning differential, architecture patterns, inheritance effects) but is excluded from freedom-hypothesis comparisons, scatter plots, and cross-round quality aggregation. Rate limits forced the move to serial eval execution (gad-62 → gad-67) after round 3's parallel attempt lost all three runs simultaneously.

glossary.short: A run that was interrupted by an Anthropic API server error (e.g. HTTP 529 overloaded_error). Different cause than rate limit, same exclusion policy.
glossary.full: Server-side transient errors (HTTP 529 overloaded_error being the most common) are distinct from account rate limits: different cause, different retry semantics, different interpretation. The TRACE.json flag `timing.api_interrupted = true` with an `interruption_note` field distinguishes them. Both rate-limited and api-interrupted runs are preserved-but-excluded. GAD v10 was api-interrupted twice; investigating the 529 pattern is a queued task before the GAD v11 retry.

glossary.short: A single attempt by an agent to satisfy an eval's requirements. Identified by project + version, e.g. escape-the-dungeon-bare/v5.
glossary.full: An eval run is the atomic unit of evaluation. Each run produces a source tree, a build, a TRACE.json, and (ideally) a human review. Multiple runs can share a round (same requirements version) but differ in agent, workflow, or retry attempt. Runs are versioned monotonically per project: escape-the-dungeon-bare/v1, v2, ..., v5. Preserved runs land under evals/<project>/<version>/ and their builds under apps/portfolio/public/evals/<project>/<version>/. The preservation contract (gad-38) is enforced by tests.

glossary.short: A set of eval runs sharing the same requirements-document version. A new requirements version = a new round.
glossary.full: Per gad-72, a round is defined by its requirements-document version. Round 1 = requirements v1 = escape-the-dungeon v1-v5 runs. Round 2 = v2 = etd v6/v7 + bare v1/v2 + emergent v1. Round 3 = v3. Round 4 = v4. Multiple runs can live inside a single round (different workflows, retries). Rounds are progressively more complex — requirements expand monotonically. Earlier rounds can be re-evaluated with new hypotheses; a new hypothesis can start a new round against any requirements version (e.g. 'does codex cli build similarly to claude code against v4' is a valid new round against v4, producing a new line of data).

glossary.short: GAD's three foundational skills — create-skill, merge-skill, find-skills — that enable the emergent-evolution hypothesis to work.
glossary.full: Per gad-73, GAD provides a triumvirate of fundamental skills: find-skills (locate a trusted fundamental from the GAD ecosystem — like 'scientific-method' or 'debug'), merge-skill (fuse a fundamental into a project-attuned skill), and create-skill (author genuinely new skills when merging isn't possible). Together they let an emergent agent evolve its skill library across rounds by finding relevant fundamentals, tailoring them to the current project, and creating new primitives only when necessary. This is the meta-layer of the in-game rune/spell merging mechanic — the agent's skill library evolves via the same combine-fundamentals pattern the escape-the-dungeon game teaches players.

glossary.short: A fundamental GAD skill that fuses a foundational skill (e.g. scientific-method) with a project context to produce a tailored variant.
glossary.full: merge-skill is one of the three fundamental skills (gad-73). Input: a foundational skill from the trusted pool + a project context. Output: a new skill that carries the foundational's rigor into the project's vocabulary. Example: merging 'scientific-method' into a kaplay rune-spellcrafting project produces 'scientific-method-for-kaplay-rune-spellcrafting' — same underlying practice, tailored examples, tailored pitfalls. This is how the emergent workflow is supposed to evolve: not by inventing new skills from scratch but by taking fundamentals and attuning them.

glossary.short: Eval metrics collected by tooling (hooks, traces, parsers) rather than by agent self-report or human judgment.
glossary.full: Per gad-69, every eval metric must answer 'can this be collected programmatically?' before 'how do we score it?'. The goal is to minimize agent-self-report (which is suspect) and minimize human-judgment (which doesn't scale) without sacrificing the dimensions that genuinely need a human. Current programmatic metrics: tool_use_mix, skill_to_tool_ratio, plan_adherence_delta, commit rhythm, produced_artifact_density. Gaps ranked in .planning/GAPS.md — the top three are skill-trigger coverage, build exit code, and skill-inheritance hygiene, all based on trace events rather than UI visual tests.

glossary.short: Hand-curated content files under vendor/get-anything-done/data/*.json, read at prebuild time and typed into the site.
glossary.full: Per gad-71, the project uses a JSON pseudo-database for hand-curated content that doesn't belong in .planning/ XML (agent process state) or evals/ TRACE.json (eval outputs). data/ holds open-questions.json, bugs.json, glossary.json, etc. — anything a human authors that the site should render. The prebuild script reads each file, validates shape, and emits typed TypeScript exports into lib/eval-data.generated.ts. No runtime services, no ACID, no servers — fine because the site has no customers, no PII, no secrets. Migration path to a real database is clear if we ever need one (we don't expect to).

glossary.short: The agent loop where everything the agent needs to remember lives in repo files, not in the agent's context window.
glossary.full: Named after the RepoPlanner predecessor to GAD and, informally, the Simpsons character who is 'special.' The idea: context windows are ephemeral, agents forget, but the repo is persistent. If the plan, the state, the decisions, the skill library all live in committed files, the agent can rebuild its working memory at any time via a single snapshot command. GAD inherits this architecture from RepoPlanner and GSD. The pattern solves the context-rot problem: decisions drift over long sessions because the agent forgets what it decided earlier — but if decisions live in DECISIONS.xml, they're always queryable.

glossary.short: snapshot → pick one task → implement → update planning docs → commit. The durable iteration cycle GAD enforces.
glossary.full: Per gad-18, the GAD loop is: (1) `gad snapshot` to re-hydrate context, (2) pick one task (status=planned) from the snapshot, (3) implement it, (4) update TASK-REGISTRY.xml (mark done), STATE.xml (next-action), DECISIONS.xml (if new decisions), (5) commit. The loop is designed to survive auto-compaction: after the agent's context is compacted mid-session, running snapshot re-loads everything it needs to continue. Subagents working on GAD-tracked projects run snapshot at the start of their work too, with the project ID passed in the agent prompt.

tasks.goal: Create .planning/ scaffold for get-anything-done and register in planning-config.toml.

Migrate gad-01 through gad-15 decisions from root DECISIONS.xml into vendor/get-anything-done/.planning/DECISIONS.xml. Add gad-16 (no subagents until evaluated) and gad-17 (context handoff via planning docs).
Add gad pack command that bundles .planning data into a repo-planner-compatible planning pack format (JSON bundle with state, phases, tasks, decisions, requirements, docRefs).
Create vendor/get-anything-done/AGENTS.md using GAD's own workflow — gad CLI commands, planning loop, context re-hydration, skill references.
Create skill stubs: skills/write-intent/SKILL.md, skills/write-feature-doc/SKILL.md, skills/write-tech-doc/SKILL.md.
Update gadConfig.load() to read [[docs.projects]] from planning-config.toml and expose as config.docsProjects. Update gad sink status and gad refs to include docs.projects entries.
Create docs-map-reader.cjs and DOCS-MAP.xml per planning root. Extend gad refs to include docs-map entries. Update gad sink compile to handle DOCS-MAP.xml.

Create eval documentation in sink: apps/portfolio/content/docs/get-anything-done/evals.mdx covering what GAD evals are, how they work, the DEFINITIONS.md model, cli-efficiency results, portfolio-bare concept.
Update AGENTS.md in all vendor project roots to reference XML planning files and gad: commands exclusively.
Run gad:eval-run on GAD itself — portfolio-bare with get-anything-done requirements as the project. Measure planning loop fidelity on GAD's own workflow.
Evaluate subagent utility formally: design an eval comparing single-session vs subagent execution on a representative task. Record findings in DECISIONS.xml (may revise gad-16).
Add decisions gad-17 (revised: auto-compact over fresh sessions), gad-18 (canonical loop: snapshot → work → update → commit), gad-19 (GSD is dead, GAD only).
Delete .claude/commands/gsd/ directory entirely (30 command files).

Rebrand .claude/hooks/ for GAD: remove gsd-check-update.js (checks npm for get-shit-done-cc). Rename gsd-context-monitor.js → context-monitor.js, strip GSD references from warning messages. Rename gsd-statusline.js → statusline.js, strip GSD update check and branding. Update .claude/settings.json to reference new hook filenames.

Simplify root AGENTS.md: remove the 15-file mandatory read order. Agents use gad snapshot on session start, not manual file reads. Remove GSD section. Keep build/lint/verify gates, conventions, site copy guide. Reference vendor/get-anything-done/AGENTS.md as the canonical loop definition.

Simplify .planning/AGENTS.md: remove redundant loop definition, trigger phrase table, and pattern examples that duplicate root AGENTS.md and vendor GAD AGENTS.md. Keep XML schema reference and planning-config.toml pointers. Reference gad-18 for the loop.

Update vendor/get-anything-done/AGENTS.md: mark as canonical loop source per gad-18. Update "Current work" section to reference phase 12. Fix gad-17 reference to reflect auto-compact over fresh sessions.

Uninstall GSD globally: delete ~/.claude/get-shit-done/, ~/.claude/commands/gsd/ (56 files), ~/.claude/hooks/gsd-*.js (5 files). Clean ~/.claude/settings.json — remove all GSD hooks and statusline, keep permissions and preferences.

Install all 56 GAD commands from vendor/get-anything-done/commands/gad/ as Claude Code slash commands in .claude/commands/gad/. Includes the full planning loop: plan-phase, execute-phase, discuss-phase, progress, check-todos, next, plus project lifecycle, debug, health, verify, stats, and all other commands.

Clean all GSD/get-shit-done references in vendor/get-anything-done/commands/gad/*.md and propagate to .claude/commands/gad/. Replace GSD→GAD, get-shit-done→get-anything-done, /gsd:→/gad: throughout. Note: 115 references to get-shit-done remain as literal directory/file paths (the actual folder is named that).
Extend gad docs command: add `gad docs list --projectid <id>` that shows all docs for a project — planning files from sink status, DOCS-MAP entries, and docs.projects entries. Add --all flag.

Create DOCS-MAP.xml for get-anything-done with entries for evals.mdx and any other non-planning feature docs in the sink. Reader (docs-map-reader.cjs) already exists — this creates the actual data files.
Create DOCS-MAP.xml for grime-time, repo-planner, and any other roots that have feature docs in their sink paths. Check each root's sink directory for non-planning MDX files.
Flesh out write-intent SKILL.md: add concrete examples, template output, edge cases. The skill should be usable by any agent reading it cold — currently too abstract.
Flesh out write-feature-doc SKILL.md: add concrete examples of good feature docs, MDX frontmatter template, sink path resolution logic. Currently steps are correct but thin.
Flesh out write-tech-doc SKILL.md: add concrete examples, mermaid diagram patterns, decision-referencing conventions. Currently steps are correct but thin.
CLI error hardening: audit all gad.cjs commands for gad-12 compliance (missing-arg UX prints choices + rerun hint). Test edge cases: missing .planning/ dirs, malformed XML, empty files.
Run gad sink compile to sync the 7 stale files (get-anything-done and grime-time). Verify sink is fully up to date after phase 12 changes.

Create a gad eval project "escape-the-dungeon" from GAMEPLAY-DESIGN.xml and STAT-AND-BEHAVIOUR-TAXONOMY.md. Convert these source docs into a GAD REQUIREMENTS.xml in the eval project directory (evals/escape-the-dungeon/). The requirements should be structured for an agent to plan phases and implement from — not a raw XML dump.

Create bin/gad-tools.cjs — the GAD equivalent of gsd-tools.cjs. Provides the core commands that workflows depend on: init (phase-op, execute-phase, plan-phase), commit, config-get, config-set, state (load, update, record-session), phase (find, add, complete), roadmap (get-phase, analyze), generate-slug, current-timestamp, scaffold, agent-skills. Reads from XML planning files instead of MD. This replaces all gsd-tools.cjs references in workflows.

Define the eval trace format for real implementations: track which gad commands were called, which gad skills were triggered, arguments passed, token counts, time-to-completion. Extend TRACE.json schema beyond the current unit-coverage model to include tool_calls[] and skill_triggers[].

Define the eval scoring rubric for real implementations: CLI efficiency (same as v6), skill trigger accuracy (were the right skills triggered at the right time), planning quality (task coverage, state hygiene), and time-to-completion. Human feedback is a separate manual score added after.

Run the escape-the-dungeon eval: fresh agent session, agent reads requirements, plans phases, implements using the GAD loop, traces everything. Produce TRACE.json and SCORE.md. This validates the full loop end-to-end on a real project.
Run the portfolio-bare eval with updated tracing. Compare against prior runs. Tighten portfolio-bare based on findings from the escape-the-dungeon eval.

Review CONTEXT.md: what it is, how discuss-phase produces it, how downstream agents (researcher, planner) consume it. Evaluate whether gad CLI should have a `gad context-doc` command or if gad-tools.cjs scaffold is sufficient. Decide if CONTEXT.md should be a GAD skill output or a planning file type. Update skills and CLI to integrate CONTEXT.md into the loop if valuable.

Create eval template AGENTS.md that enforces the GAD loop. The eval template's .planning/ directory must include an AGENTS.md that tells the agent: (1) run gad snapshot first, (2) plan phases in ROADMAP.xml before coding, (3) update TASK-REGISTRY.xml per task, (4) update STATE.xml after each phase, (5) capture decisions. This file is what the agent reads to know how to work — not a prompt instruction it ignores.

Update eval-run skill/command to inject snapshot context INTO the agent prompt. Instead of telling the agent "run gad snapshot", the eval-run workflow should run the snapshot itself and include the output in the agent's initial context. The agent starts with context, not instructions to get context.

Create gad eval trace reconstruct --project <name> command. Parses git log + planning doc state to build TRACE.json after the fact. Detects: which files were created/modified per commit, which planning docs were updated, time between commits (phase duration), task completion count from TASK-REGISTRY.xml diff. This replaces manual log-cmd/log-skill with post-hoc reconstruction.

Add checkpoint enforcement to execute-phase workflow. Before the agent can start the next task, it must have: (1) updated TASK-REGISTRY.xml for the previous task, (2) updated STATE.xml next-action. The workflow checks these preconditions. If not met, it prompts the agent to update before continuing. Not a hook — a step in the workflow that reads the planning docs and verifies.

Auto-scaffold CONVENTIONS.md after first implementation phase of greenfield projects. When phase 01 (or first phase with code output) completes in a project with no existing CONVENTIONS.md, run a lightweight codebase scan (file structure, naming patterns, import style, content pack format) and write .planning/CONVENTIONS.md. Integrate with gad:map-codebase skill output.

Define expected_triggers for implementation evals. The TRACE.json skill_accuracy field needs expected_triggers populated before the eval runs — defining which skills SHOULD fire and when. Create a template expected_triggers list for implementation evals: discuss-phase before planning, plan-phase before execution, execute-phase per phase, verify-work after phases, map-codebase after first implementation.

Re-run escape-the-dungeon eval with fixes applied (template AGENTS.md, snapshot injection, checkpoint enforcement). Compare v3 TRACE.json against v2. Validate that: gad CLI is actually used, task-level tracking happens, conventions are generated, trace has real data not backfilled.
Move get-shit-done/workflows/ → workflows/ at GAD repo top level. All 58 workflow .md files.
Move get-shit-done/templates/ → templates/ at GAD repo top level. All 34 template files.
Move get-shit-done/references/ → references/ at GAD repo top level. All 15 reference files.
Update all 42 command @-path references from get-shit-done/workflows/ → workflows/, get-shit-done/templates/ → templates/, get-shit-done/references/ → references/. Sync to .claude/commands/gad/.
Delete get-shit-done/ directory entirely (bin/, lib/, workflows/, templates/, references/). gsd-tools.cjs replaced by gad-tools.cjs. Verify no remaining references.
Copy eval v3 game build (dist/) to apps/portfolio/public/evals/escape-the-dungeon/ for docs site hosting. Verify it's servable as static page.

Review RepoPlanner sprint model: read scripts/loop-cli.mjs sprint commands (set-size, show, context), understand how sprintSize groups phases, how context sprint filters to sprint-scoped paths and task counts. Document what RP gives that GAD doesn't.

Add sprint concept to GAD: configurable sprint size in planning-config.toml (default 5 phases). Add gad sprint show (list sprint boundaries) and gad sprint context (sprint-scoped paths + summary) commands to gad.cjs.

Add gad snapshot --sprint mode: collapse done phases to id|title|done (no goal text), inline only active/planned phases within current sprint, show only open tasks (planned/in-progress), omit completed task history. Measure token reduction vs current full dump.

Add file refs to snapshot: for each active/in-progress task, include code and doc files touched (from git log scoped to project path). Add recent git log (last 5-10 commits for the project). These give the agent immediate code context without manual file reads.

Add file refs to TASK-REGISTRY.xml schema: optional <files> block per task listing paths touched during implementation. Update gad-tools.cjs and any task-completion workflows to prompt for file list on task done.
Measure optimized snapshot token count. Target: ~3,000 tokens for get-anything-done (currently ~9,750). Run cli-efficiency eval v7 with sprint-scoped snapshot. Compare composite score vs v6.
Update docs/context-budget.md with new snapshot token counts and sprint context window analysis. Update DEFINITIONS.md if sprint changes the information unit model.
Run gad:reverse-engineer on vendor/repub-builder/src/reader to produce REQUIREMENTS.xml, DECISIONS.xml, CONVENTIONS.md, ROADMAP.xml for the reader-workspace eval.
Create reader-workspace eval project with REQUIREMENTS.md, template/, and AGENTS.md.
Run reader-workspace eval v1: fresh agent builds the EPUB reader from reverse-engineered requirements alone. Produce TRACE.json and SCORE.md. Validates the full reverse-engineer → implement cycle.
Score reader-workspace v1: requirement coverage (51 criteria), architecture alignment (12 decisions), state hygiene, convention adherence. Compare output against reference implementation quality.

Replace the stub in `gad eval run` with a prompt generator: read the eval template (AGENTS.md, REQUIREMENTS.xml, DECISIONS.xml, CONVENTIONS.md, ROADMAP.xml, STATE.xml), construct a full bootstrap prompt, and output it to stdout + eval-output.txt. The user/agent copies this prompt into their AI tool. Agent-agnostic per gad-01.

Add `gad eval suite` CLI command: lists all eval projects with templates, generates bootstrap prompts for each, outputs them as separate files in a timestamped suite run directory. Shows a summary table of what will be run.

Add `gad eval report` cross-project comparison: reads TRACE.json from latest run of each project, produces a comparison table (phases, tasks, discipline, scores) across all projects. Supports --all flag for all projects or specific --project list.

Add comprehensive logging: GAD CLI call logger (JSONL per day), PostToolUse hook for tool tracing (Bash/Read/Write/Edit/Agent/Skill/Grep/Glob), `gad log show|stats|clear` commands, `gad eval trace from-log` for definitive trace building from actual logs, GAD_LOG_DIR env for eval-scoped logs.
Run the full eval suite: portfolio-bare v3, escape-the-dungeon v4, reader-workspace v2. Launch all three in parallel worktree agents. Reconstruct traces and produce cross-project report.
Iterate on findings: review eval results, tighten at least one skill based on patterns, update eval-suite SKILL.md with lessons learned, add any new eval criteria discovered.

Document tooling eval contract: extend TRACE.json with eval_type tooling|mcp, trace_schema_version, nested tooling or mcp metrics blocks, optional metrics.json for granular histograms. Record gad-26–gad-28 in DECISIONS.xml. Add a short section to vendor/get-anything-done/AGENTS.md (or evals/README) describing implementation vs tooling evals and how regressions are compared.

Implement `gad dev` or `gad watch planning`: debounced watch on .planning/**/*.xml (configurable), invoke existing refs verify logic, print one JSON summary line per run (ok, missing paths, timing) for scripts and terminal/CI consumers. Optional --poll for unreliable FS watchers. No network by default.

Add evals/tooling-watch-baseline (or equivalent): fixed fixture repo with known good/bad paths, automated Node harness that starts watcher, performs scripted edits, asserts correctness + latency percentiles, writes evals/<project>/vN/TRACE.json with eval_type tooling. Runnable in CI without an LLM.

Harden implementation eval scoring automation: generate scores.json alongside SCORE.md from score-generator; change gad eval scores to prefer JSON totals over SCORE.md regex. Prepare same pattern for tooling composite (tooling_composite) so weights can evolve without breaking old runs.

Optional MCP server package: thin adapter over the same core as CLI (verify, snapshot summary, session status). Stdio MCP; document Cursor config. Agents may use it; primary user remains human IDE workflows.
MCP tooling eval: golden tool invocations, cold-start and per-tool latency, error rate on bad args. Produces eval_type mcp TRACE.json; compare across gad releases.
IDE integration: document VS Code / Cursor Tasks (background watch), optional problem matcher mapping JSON errors to Problems panel. Update portfolio sink evals.mdx when behavior is stable.

Extend gad eval report and gad eval diff to include tooling/mcp runs (filter by eval_type, compare tooling.metrics and composite). Ensure suite or follow-up command can run implementation + tooling evals in one report.
Fix MCP UTF-8 buffer bug: Content-Length counts bytes but handleData used string.length. tools/list responses with em dashes broke the parser. Switch to Buffer throughout MCP harness and server.
Speed up planning-ref-verify by reading from planning-config roots instead of full tree walk, plus cache fs.statSync results.

Standardize implementation eval composite formula. Replace inconsistent per-eval weights with a single v3 formula: requirement_coverage + planning_quality + per_task_discipline + skill_accuracy + time_efficiency + human_review. Document in DEFINITIONS.md.
Clean skill_accuracy: remove methodology behaviors (decisions-captured, multi-phase-planning, phase-completion, task-lifecycle) from expected_triggers. Only actual /gad:* skill invocations count.
Add gad eval review CLI command to record human review scores that get folded into the composite with the v3 weights.
Add gad eval open CLI command to serve eval build output via local HTTP server (file:// doesn't work for ES module bundles from Vite/KAPLAY).
Create escape-the-dungeon-bare eval project: same game requirements, no GAD framework, agent creates own workflow. Baseline A/B comparison for the freedom hypothesis.
Create escape-the-dungeon-emergent eval project: no framework, but inherits skills from the previous run. Tests whether self-created systems improve iteratively.
Version game requirements. Document v1→v2→v3 history inside REQUIREMENTS.xml. Each eval round should reference the requirements version it used.

Build requirements v3: add gate criteria (game loop, spell crafting, UI quality) that override requirement_coverage to 0 if failed. Trim source gameplay design doc from 640 → 120 lines for the vertical slice focus.
Run round 2 greenfield evals with v2 requirements. Three conditions: GAD v7, Bare v2, Emergent v1.
Run round 3 greenfield evals with v3 requirements (gates). GAD v8, Bare v3, Emergent v2.
Document the freedom hypothesis finding: GAD framework constraint correlates inversely with creative output quality on greenfield game implementation. Capture in FINDINGS doc and decision.
Build eval preservation contract: gad eval preserve command that copies code+planning+build+logs to canonical per-version locations. Enforce via tests/eval-preservation.test.cjs.
Fix eval preserve to copy the ENTIRE project (not cherry-picked files). Agent may create files we don't anticipate.
Mandate game/.planning/ as the home for all eval workflow artifacts regardless of framework. Bare and emergent must put workflow files there too, not at project root.
Add gad worktree management commands: list, show, clean, prune. User had no visibility into .claude/worktrees/agent-* directories.
Categorize evals by eval_mode (greenfield|brownfield) and workflow (gad|bare|emergent). Show in gad eval list.

Create three brownfield eval projects (etd-brownfield-gad/bare/emergent) using bare v3 as baseline. Shared extension requirements (_brownfield-extensions-v4.md) describing v4 features (floor progression, respawning encounters, forge affinity, skills system, visual upgrade).
Port session tracking into TASK-REGISTRY.xml instead of Claude Code Task tool. Stop using ephemeral session tasks for GAD-tracked work.
Create an experiment log doc (evals/EXPERIMENT-LOG.md) that tracks every round: conditions, requirements version, results, findings. Running ledger across all rounds.

Build requirements v4: pressure-oriented design (not features checklist). Authored dungeon with floors → 5-8 rooms → boss gate. Room types: Combat, Elite, Forge, Rest, Event. Each floor must introduce a mechanical constraint that can't be brute-forced with default spells. Forge is gate with at least one encounter per floor that favors crafted/adapted spells.
